title: Cross-Product Channel Exploration with Stolen Card Credentials
narrative: Fraudster tests a compromised card across multiple product categories (C, H, R, S, W) that the legitimate cardholder has never accessed. The card credential itself passes verification checks—CVV correct, billing address matches—but card-level counters reveal the card is suddenly being used for product types outside its historical pattern. A card historically used only for physical goods (ProductCD=W) suddenly appears in digital content purchases (ProductCD=C) and service transactions (ProductCD=S) within the same rolling window. Individual transaction amounts and timing appear normal, masking the fraud, but the diversity of ProductCD values accessed by card1 in recent history (C14) spikes above the cardholder's established baseline. This pattern exploits the fact that ProductCD diversity is not typically validated at authorization time, allowing fraudsters to monetize credentials across channels before detection.
indicators: C14 >= 3.0 indicating card used across three or more distinct ProductCD categories
C1 > 12.0 (above p75) showing elevated card-counter aggregation across contexts
card1_distinct_products >= 3 with card history showing <= 1 ProductCD in prior 90 days
D1 >= 1 indicating device-card pairing exists but ProductCD for this pairing is new
C8 > 6.0 (above p75) reflecting increased card activity across merchant categories
TransactionAmt between 50 and 200, fitting typical cardholder amount distribution
indicator_explanations: Card is suddenly appearing in three or more different product categories within a short window
Card-level activity counter shows usage frequency well above the cardholder's normal baseline
Card has accessed multiple product types despite historical pattern of single-category usage
Device has been seen with this card before but never for this product category
Card activity across merchant types has increased beyond the established cardholder pattern
Transaction amount fits the cardholder's spending range to avoid triggering amount-based alerts
summary: Compromised card credentials are being used to make purchases across multiple ProductCD categories that fall outside the cardholder's historical usage pattern, while individual transaction characteristics appear normal.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders typically establish consistent product-category patterns (e.g., physical goods only, or digital content only). A sudden spike in ProductCD diversity combined with elevated card counters indicates credential theft, as fraudsters test the card's validity across multiple monetization channels before the cardholder notices unauthorized activity.
